title: US-China Chip Export Controls
description: The ongoing evolution of US chip export control policy toward China. From the first round of restrictions in 2022 to the 2026 pivot toward advanced packaging technology — tracking every policy shift, bilateral response, and impact on the global semiconductor supply chain.

timeline.title: US Commerce Dept Expands Entity List — 14 Chinese Semiconductor Firms Added
timeline.summary: The latest entity list update targets EDA toolchains and photoresist suppliers, marking the first time design software companies have been included. China's Commerce Ministry issued a statement of strong opposition the same day.

timeline.title: Netherlands and Japan Simultaneously Tighten DUV Equipment Export Licenses to China
timeline.summary: ASML and Tokyo Electron suspended delivery of DUV equipment maintenance services to China under pressure from their respective governments. Industry estimates suggest this affects roughly 30% of China's planned mature-node capacity expansion.

timeline.title: Huawei Launches Ascend 910C — Performance Approaches A100, But Packaging Still Relies on External Foundries
timeline.summary: Huawei used a chiplet architecture to work around compute restrictions, but advanced packaging still depends on specific foundry partners. Analysts noted this exposed the exact lever for the next phase of controls.

timeline.title: BIS Internal Draft Leaked: Controls Shift from Compute Thresholds to Packaging Technology
timeline.summary: A draft obtained by The Information shows the US is redefining its export control framework — anchoring new restrictions on advanced packaging techniques such as CoWoS and InFO rather than FLOPS thresholds.

timeline.title: US Commerce Dept Formally Publishes Revised Export Control Framework
timeline.summary: The new framework targets advanced packaging rather than raw compute. Reuters described it as 'narrowed in scope'; South China Morning Post and multiple industry analysts called it a 'precision upgrade'. China's Commerce Ministry issued a reciprocal countermeasure statement within 24 hours.

timeline.title: China Urges US to Drop Section 301 Probe 5 Days Before Trump-Xi Summit
timeline.summary: At a Washington hearing, Beijing called the US Section 301 excess-capacity investigation legally flawed and urged its withdrawal before the May 14-15 summit. Analysts expect the leaders to announce Chinese purchases of US goods and a Board of Trade framework, but underlying chip/export-control tensions remain unresolved. Chinese exports to US fell 11% YoY in early 2026.

timeline.title: Trump-Xi Summit T-3 Days: USAF C-17 Transports in Beijing; Boeing & Citigroup CEOs Confirmed; CFR Warns China Holds Upper Hand
timeline.summary: With the summit 72 hours away, four USAF C-17 transports have landed at Beijing Capital with the presidential motorcade and security equipment. Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg and Citigroup's CEO confirmed they will accompany Trump. Reporting from Fortune (Steve Hanke) and CFR argues China has spent six years stockpiling leverage — rare-earth dominance, Hormuz-linked oil exposure, and an elevated Taiwan agenda. Expected deliverables remain the Board of Trade framework, Chinese soybean (25M tons/year × 3) and Boeing purchase commitments, and possible easing of 14nm/7nm chip-equipment export controls. The Iran war is the unresolved precondition Beijing has flagged, but the White House is proceeding regardless.

timeline.title: Trump-Xi Beijing Summit T-2 Days — US Sanctions Iran-China Oil Traders as Summit Tensions Mount
timeline.summary: With the summit now 48 hours away, the US sanctioned 12 companies and individuals for facilitating Iranian oil sales to China — adding a new friction point to an already complex agenda. Pre-summit negotiations in Seoul sought to lock in quick deliverables. Expected summit outcomes: Board of Trade managed-trade framework, Chinese commitment to purchase ~500 Boeing aircraft, sustained US agricultural commodity imports, and possible easing of 14nm/7nm chip-equipment export controls. CFR analysts argued China holds structural leverage through rare-earth dominance, Hormuz-linked oil exposure, and elevated Taiwan positioning. The Iran war remains an unresolved precondition Beijing flagged but Washington is proceeding regardless.

timeline.title: Trump Lands in Beijing for Three-Day State Visit — Iran War Now Dominates an Agenda That Was Supposed to Be About Trade
timeline.summary: Trump arrived in Beijing on Wednesday evening for the May 13–15 state visit, the first by a US president in nearly nine years. Welcome ceremony and bilateral meeting with Xi scheduled for Thursday morning, followed by a Temple of Heaven tour and state banquet. The Iran war has crowded out the originally framed trade-and-chips agenda: US officials confirmed Trump will press Xi on Chinese purchases of Iranian oil and possible arms transfers to Tehran. Chinese readouts signal Beijing will press for an explicit US statement opposing (not just 'not supporting') Taiwan independence. The US executive delegation includes Tesla CEO Elon Musk, Apple CEO Tim Cook and Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg. Washington Post, TIME and CFR all warn Xi holds the structural upper hand from six years of accumulated leverage; the summit is more likely to deliver symbolic stabilization than structural reform.

timeline.title: Trump-Xi Bilateral Day Opens in Beijing — Boeing Megadeal Watch (Kalshi 86%), Iran Pressure, and Taiwan Declaratory Policy Are Today's Three Levers
timeline.summary: Substantive day of the state visit: welcome ceremony, formal bilateral meeting, Temple of Heaven tour, state banquet. Kalshi prices an 86% probability of a Boeing aircraft purchase announcement; sources put the order at 500–600 737 MAX jets, potentially the largest commercial aircraft deal in history. The US delegation includes Tim Cook (Apple), Elon Musk (Tesla/SpaceX), Jensen Huang (NVIDIA), Kelly Ortberg (Boeing), Larry Fink (BlackRock), and Citigroup's CEO. Trump confirmed Iran will be a major topic; Beijing wants an explicit US statement opposing Taiwan independence, and $14B in pending US arms sales to Taipei sit in limbo. CFR/Brookings/Washington Post consensus: realistic ceiling is a tariff-truce extension plus headline commercial commitments — structural decoupling unchanged. Markets rebounded on summit optimism: Nasdaq +1.20%, S&P 500 +0.58% to 7,414.

timeline.title: Trump Departs Beijing With 200-Jet Boeing Order (Half Expected) and 'Strategic Stability' Framework — Xi Issues Bluntest Taiwan Warning of His Tenure
timeline.summary: State visit concludes Friday. Headline deliverables: 200 Boeing jets (Trump on Fox: 'Boeing wanted 150, they got 200'; analysts had expected 500–600 — Boeing shares fell 4.8% on the news), Chinese commitments to buy US soybeans, oil, and LNG, joint agreement that the Strait of Hormuz must remain open, Chinese pledge of no military equipment to Iran, and a 'constructive China-U.S. relationship of strategic stability' framework Beijing will treat as the guiding framework for the next three years. Xi paired the friendly optics with the bluntest Taiwan warning of his tenure: 'The Taiwan question is the most important issue in China-U.S. relations. If it is handled properly, the bilateral relationship will enjoy overall stability. Otherwise, the two countries will have clashes and even conflicts, putting the entire relationship in great jeopardy.' The structural files — advanced chips, Section 301, $11B Taiwan arms package authorized in December but not delivered — were not resolved. CFR/Brookings read: realistic ceiling reached, structural decoupling unchanged.

timeline.title: Next-Day Verdicts on the Beijing Summit: Foreign Policy's 'Few Wins for Trump,' Time's 'Power Has Shifted East,' Euronews 'Underwhelming' — Trump Invites Xi for September Washington Visit
timeline.summary: Trump returned to the United States Friday after the two-day state visit. Next-day analysis converged: Foreign Policy headlined 'From Iran to Trade, China Summit Produces Few Wins for Trump,' Time ran 'Trump's China Trip Underscores How Power Has Shifted East,' and Euronews called the outcome 'underwhelming.' The 200-jet Boeing announcement (versus 500–600 expected — Boeing closed -4.8%) remained the only major deal announced. Trump invited Xi for a return state visit to Washington in September. The US readout omitted Taiwan entirely; the Chinese readout placed Taiwan as 'the most important issue.' Section 301, advanced chip export controls, and the $11B Taiwan arms package authorized in December but undelivered all remain unresolved. The 'constructive strategic stability' framework Beijing announced will guide ties for 'the next three years and beyond' is being treated by CFR and Brookings as verbal architecture rather than structural reset. Within 24 hours of the joint pledge to keep the Strait of Hormuz open, two maritime incidents (Fujairah seizure and Haji Ali sinking) tested whether the framework has any operational teeth.
